speaker: interviewer
text: Ernest Bronstein, tape number three. Mr. Bronstein, so, in 1949 what happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949, like I said, it started to get very bad situation for myself. They was after me that I was black marketing. And I decided it's better for me to get out of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I find out in the next transport within a few days heading to United States. I had an aunt here at the time, you know. They sent me some paper, but it wasn't enough. But I had enough connection at the time for the joint and the transport, so they okayed me to get to the next transport to come to United States, which I happen to remember the ship name was [INAUDIBLE]. That was the name of the ship that they put us in, you know, and came to the United States. And so how long from the time you decided to come to the United States were you on a ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on the ship, I would say, probably about eight, nine, or 10 days we was coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean, you decide to go to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many people had to wait months once they made the decision.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have to wait?

speaker: interviewee
text: About one week.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, you know, I had no choice. I felt, you know, I better get out of there fast, you know. And I was preparing myself ahead of time. I talked to people, and I always said, next time, you know, next time. And I always had a pretty good time in Frankfurt. You know, so I stood along. But when it started to get a little bit heat, it was time for me to move. And it took me one week to get out of there. And when I arrived to the United State, you know, like I said, I don't have too much papers. So they took me to Ellis Island, which it was a Friday night when we arrived. And they had no court or anything Friday night. So, but, you know, Ellis Island, you know, when people talk about it, it wasn't like a concentration camp. You know, I was treated very well, was getting good food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I might add that it was a very, very nice incident that happened to me. And it was a PX, with a lot of all kind of goodies, chocolate. And you can buy everything, you know, but I don't have a dime in my pocket. And next to me was a gentleman, and he see me looking. He said, you would like to have some? And he gave me a dollar. I'll never forget in my life, he give me a dollar to buy stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, you know, for a nickel, you could buy a bar of chocolate. So I spend a dollar, which I was really going out of life to have that kind of stuff. So I enjoyed it, you know. And I was there till Friday night, Saturday and Sunday. And Monday morning, they took me into the court. And they asked me what happened. And I told them. I was playing soccer. I showed them pictures you know, with my soccer playing. And yes, I said, I have an aunt here. And within half hour, you know, I was released, you know. And at that time, my brother had a friend in New York, and he was waiting-- they was waiting for me to get out of there. And I went to them, you know, for three days. And after three days, you know, I took a train, you know, and I was coming to California to meet my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother at that time was in California?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrive in California, and then what happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I arrive in California. My brother was waiting for me, you know. And, as you know, matter of weeks, you know, I went to work. And I never forget I went to work-- for 75 cents I was getting an hour in a cabinet maker. It was that time, you know, I think a lot of people might recall it. You know, it was very hard to get a job. And I was looking-- there was in the paper, a cabinet maker with experience. Well I can imagine I went there as a cabinet maker, which I never had a hammer in my life in my hand or a machine to know how to work. But, anyway, they hired me. And I worked there for, I would say, about two, three months till during the lunchtime, I was pushing the buttons to see how to start the machines. But after three months, you know, I said I can't do this kind of work, so they let me go. And the place where I was renting a little room from a family, his daughter was going with another guy who was also in concentration camp. But he already was here two years. And his uncle, which called [? Rowen ?] and [? Spiegel ?], he was building houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I met him and I ask him if he would give me a job. So he gave me a job to work, you know, in construction business. Well, what I did over there, they ask me if I drive a truck and I said, yes I do. But never ask me if I have a license or not, so I was driving a truck for three months there without the license. So it was a little bit hard job for me and after I went to take my test to take a driving license. And this is another incident that I has to tell you, that I went into an office where taking the driver's license test. I was getting that long paper which I supposed to fill out. And I went next to another gentleman who was filling out the paper. And I saw his number was number five and mine was number four. So I went back to the window, and I asked the woman, over there, the lady, I said, will you please give me a number five? So the lady look at me, but-- and she gave me a number five. So I went back to the gentleman next to it, and I started to copy what he did. I did the same thing. Well, I didn't understand the question.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about two minutes later, somebody come behind me and picked me out and said, you come with me. So he took me into an office. And I start to explain that I didn't understand the question. Yes, I was looking what he was doing. So you can't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what he did, he put a big map out of me with little cars. And that's the way I took my test, my driving test. And he said, you drive a car? And I said, sure I drive a car. I've been with a car over there. But I've hit my mind out of it, he said, who's car is that? I said, a friend of mine. You know, but he came with me, and I don't know what it is. He said, can you drive this car? I said sure. So he sit down in the car, and I sit down in the car, and started to drive. I didn't even went two blocks, he says to me, you go back. You know, I don't need. You drive. You know what you're doing. So that's the way I took my driver's license in the United States. So I went, and I was about another month. I was all together I worked for about four months. But I said, that's not for me. And I looked in the paper and I find that LA Pleating and Button Company needed a driver to pick up materials. And I didn't know at the time what was it, but I thought it's better than drive a truck, you know, and work in construction. That was downtown in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to work over there. They hired me. And I worked as a delivery boy. I went to factories where they make dresses, skirts, and blouses, and so on. And I went to pick up the orders. About six months later, I tell my boss that, listen, I would like to be a salesman and not a driver. He said, no, you can't even speak the English. I said, listen, you're paying seven percent at the time for the other salesman who work for you. So you give me five percent, you know. If not, you know, I quit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had no choice, you know. He hired me as a salesman. So I had one suit in my name. So that was a Friday. And Monday morning, I went up, and I dressed up. And I went to the town, and I knocked on the doors, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went into the factories. They throw me out of the front door, arrive in the back door, and knocked the door and told them, you have to give me a chance. I'm just came from concentration camp. And I was giving them-- till they pick up a little rag, they throwed it at me. So, OK, make me half a dozen covered button. I run back to the place, and I made the button, and 15 minutes later, I told him, here is your sample. So the guy was shooked up, you know. He said I never get that kind of services. But to make it short, you know, it was the biggest competition, I'll never forget, was Davis Company. And within six months, I took 50% away his customers and brought it, you know, to our place. And my competition, you know, my salesmen, at that time, we was two of us. He was making probably 25% of the money that I was making.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the boss find out, you know, that Davis company was after me, was offer me twice as much salary than you was paying-- he was paying me, my boss at the time. So he called me in the office. He's offering me to be a partner. So I said, what do you mean, partner? I don't have money. So I went to the join, and the join loaned me $2,000 at that time. That was that I give them a deposit. We went to a lawyer, and we made the paper between us and we become partners.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to do the same thing. And I worked as a partner approximately about a year. And we decided that I'm going to get a draw till it's my share paid in full. But a year later, I had an ingrowing hair in my face. And I needed a little surgery. So I went to him and I said, listen, I need a few hundred dollar extra or I'm going to have to have a surgery. Oh no, he said, you can't have it. You know, you don't know what you signed, you know. You signed it without-- before you pay your share in full, I have no right to take out any of the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to an attorney, which at the time, already I understood a little better English. I know what I was doing. I went to an attorney Friday. I'll never forget that was a Friday afternoon. And I asked him, can I say the-- I didn't even have a paper with me. I went to his office to find out what I signed at that time. So it was true. He explained it to me. I signed that I have no rights to take any money till it's my share not paid in full. I said, OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Saturday morning, every Saturday morning, we was getting our draw. When he give me the draw, I turned the check in the side and I signed it. And I said this is the settlement between you and me. I'm quitting. And we dissolve our partnership. And that was a Saturday morning. I had $200 in my name at the time. I went and I rented the hall in the same building. And I rented machinery for $75, and I paid $75 for machinery. And I went in the contracting business. And at that time, you know, everybody was crying for work, you know, to work eight hours. And I went to the same people that I was taking away the business from the other company. I ask them to give me a chance, you know. I have good people. I got a good foreman. To give me a chance to do some contracting for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they started to give me little by little business. And I will tell you that within six months, I was working 24 hours a day. And my shop, the other shop was work-- had hardly had any work for eight days. They throw me out of one door. I went back in back door. And I was just hustling when I can and I was working very hard. So I work myself up, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working as a contractor, approximately about a year and a half. And I decided, no, I want to go in a manufacturing business. So I had a few dollars already put together. I saved some money. I told the people, I'm sorry. I need-- I don't need anymore work but I'm going to go into my own business. I started to make samples. I called New York. I quote the people selling the material. And I started to make samples. I get people that listen. You have to give me a chance. You want to work with me. I can pay you just a little. But I had very good sample makers. I got foreman. I get everybody that I can to start to make, to work as a manufacturer. I never forget, May Company. The buyer's name was Mrs. Cooper, which somebody wants to go to see her, at the time, they have to make appointments for weeks and take the samples. But I did the same thing, you know. I find out which door Mrs. Cooper goes in in the back. I started to make my samples. When I was ready, I had under my arm. Mrs. Cooper goes in in the back door and here I am, pushing her. And I said, Mrs. Cooper, my name is so and so and I am manufacturing clothing. No, here is my sample. I throw it on the table. And she was shocked, she didn't know what to do. And I said Mrs. Cooper, please, give me a chance. Just let me show you what I have, so on. Make it short, Mrs. Cooper was giving me an order. I did the same thing with Bullochs, and with other big companies. And that's the way I started my career as a manufacturer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the manufacturing business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to say, I'm sorry, not too long. Only about a year, and I'll explain you why. You know, I didn't know too much about the business. But if you will pick up a material and you cut it in half, I will know the difference. But what happened when I made the dresses, it was 60 cotton and 40 rayon. And what happened, a lot of salesman came between, and they was offering it for $0.10 less a yard. And I cancelled this material, and I bought the other material. And I started to manufacturing it. That material, all of a sudden, they were shipping it to me back and I didn't understand why. So that was 50-50, instead of 60-40. And that's the way out of that particular business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened, you know, my CPA and my employer was pretty smart people. He said, hey, you can call a bankruptcy. You can do nothing. They can do nothing to you. Can forget it, you know, and you don't owe nobody nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, but I was smart enough to do that time. I said, no, I don't want to do that. I said, I'm going to have to pay everybody dollar for dollar. And that's the way I want to get out of the business. So I went to a bank at that time called the Crocker National-- Crocker Bank, which the loan officer's name was Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. I'll never forget it. Somebody says to just go to see him. Tell him I'll send you to him. So, anyway, I show up to Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. And I said, Mr. [INAUDIBLE], I'm going to need a loan. And I can tell you one thing. This is my two hands. This is my security. And I'll explain that why do I need a loan. That I'm getting out of a business. And then after the contracting business, where did you-- what other business did you go into then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's me tell you what happened at the time. I went-- that's the time was building the new Beverly Hilton Hotel. And a friend of mine was one of the buying manager, you know, for the hotel. And he asked me, why don't you come and work? That's the guy who was in the camp, which I told you, he was in a camp, Bergen-Belsen, but he was in the food distribution. That was the guy that he was working for the Hilton Corporation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what happened, you know, he asked me what. I go there to work. I said, what kind of work? And he said, go work as a waiter. And I end there worked as a waiter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And might as well tell you that I worked there for a while at the Hilton Hotel as a waiter till I saved enough money. And I manipulated enough money to go back and start up again to back to my own business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What business was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the car business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the car business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the car business approximately two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, two and a half years. And then, when I went out of there, my brother was in the convalesce hospital business and old age home business. And they asked me if I want to go be associated with them. In 1959, I went to work with my brother and his brother-in-law who was in that particular business. And then we started to build more convalescent, more old-aged homes, and become a partner. And that was my main career that I became an administrator. And I was running the show. And I was working for-- til 1982.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little about your family. When did you meet your wife? When did you get married? Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have. I have a daughter named Gilda. And I was married in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] let me think, to make sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I can't recall, exactly. I'm sorry. I don't recall exactly the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: No problem. So you have one daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: One daughter, yes. And two grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was retired in 1982, like I said, when I went out of the business. But, you know, I was still young. I felt, you know, and I thought I had a few hundred thousand dollars, I had a nice home. So, but it looked like the money started to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 19-- I started to go back to Europe to see you what can I do. I started to get a little export import business. But about three years ago, I find in Hungary somebody invented [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] certain things that I get involved with three years ago, which I'm doing today. And that involves?

speaker: interviewee
text: That involves particular machines that they saturated, machines that put oxygen into the water, which will be coming out within the next 30 to 60 days, will be on the market here in the United States. And we got worldwide distribution and worldwide patent on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we close, Mr. Bronstein, is there any message you would like to give to the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing, the only reason I was giving this interview, is that I don't want my children or my grandchildren or my grand-grandchildren ever forget about what really happened to the Jews, you know, in the 1930s and '40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, we wish to thank you for your time. Thank you very much Mr. Bronstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. It was my pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Bronstein, would you introduce us to your daughter, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Gilda Evans, which is my favorite daughter in the whole wide world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Your only daughter in the whole wide world. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Gilda, is there anything you would like to say about perhaps your feelings as a child of a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think just the fact that I'm here at all is something of a miracle. The fact that my children are here is a miracle. And my experience with other people I know, people who are survivors and other children of survivors who I know personally, it seems to me that the people who did survive survived on two opposite ends of the spectrum in terms of their outlook on life, their attitudes. Either they came out with this dark cloud around them that they were determined to carry with them, and unfortunately relive those horrible experiences over and over again. Or they came out loving life more than anything. And determined to enjoy life to the fullest each and every day, no matter what. And, fortunately, my father falls into the latter category. And as such, the greatest gift that he's given me, based on his outlook and his experiences, is that he is imbued me with this wonderful sense of determination, and this wonderful survivor instinct, if you will. So that no matter what happens, as difficult as life can be at times, and as hard as situations can become, I'm kind of the eternal optimist. I always believe that someway, somehow something's going to work out. Things are going to go right. I'm going to find my way out of this. I'm going to make things happen the way I want them to. And that really comes from him. That's the instinct, if you will, that he's passed along to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your husband and your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, his name is William, Will. And we have two beautiful children named Lewis and Lee.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is four and my daughter is one. And we have a wonderful relationship. [LAUGHS] And business-wise, my husband is in the computer business and I'm in the entertainment business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much again on behalf of the Shoah Foundation. We wish to thank you for your time. Mr. Bronstein, will you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken 1946 on Bergen-Belsen. I was playing soccer. And that my head, you know, that saved me a lot of time and saved my life to come even to United State.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Bronstein, will you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture is my son-in-law, William, my daughter, Gilda, and my grandson, Louis, and my granddaughter, Lee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken here in 1995 in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once again, Mr. Bronstein, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, we thank you for your time. And we hope that from here on in, you have a complete and wonderful life. Thank you again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. It was my pleasure.
